---
name: marketplace-seller-or-partner-handoff.md
description: Use when the agent is handing off a customer issue between platform support and a marketplace seller, partner, reseller, carrier, implementation partner, app developer, service provider, or other third party where authority, evidence, privacy, and follow-up ownership must be clear.
---

# Marketplace Seller Or Partner Handoff

Marketplace and partner support can easily become a maze. The customer sees one experience, while authority may be split among platform, seller, carrier, app developer, reseller, or service partner. A weak handoff creates circular referrals, repeated evidence collection, privacy leaks, and unresolved promises. This skill helps the agent move a customer issue across partner boundaries without abandoning the customer.

## Core Rules

### Identify the relationship and authority

Before handing off, determine who owns the transaction, contract, warranty, refund, fulfillment, technical fix, data access, moderation decision, or service delivery. A platform may control account access and payment rails while a seller controls shipment or product quality. A reseller may control billing while the product team controls technical support.

Do not assume the customer knows this structure. Explain authority in practical terms, not internal org language.

### Verify the customer's standing

Check whether the customer bought through the marketplace, direct site, reseller, app store, enterprise contract, third-party seller, or partner implementation. Confirm order IDs, account ownership, subscription source, region, warranty status, and seller identity before redirecting.

Incorrect routing causes delays and may disclose information to the wrong party.

### Transfer context, not just responsibility

A handoff should include the issue summary, customer goal, evidence collected, actions already taken, relevant IDs, policy basis, urgency, commitments made, and what the next owner must decide. If the partner requires the customer to initiate contact, provide a prepared summary the customer can use.

The customer should not have to retell the whole story unless identity, privacy, or consent rules require it.

### Protect customer data

Share only information the partner is allowed to receive. Consider consent, data-processing terms, marketplace policy, regulated data, payment information, private messages, health or safety details, and account-security context.

If the partner needs sensitive evidence, use the approved channel and tell the customer why it is needed. Do not paste private customer data into informal partner chats.

### Set handoff expectations

Tell the customer who will respond, through which channel, expected timing, what information they may need, and what the original support team will still track. If timing is unknown, provide a checkpoint rather than a promise.

For urgent, safety, fraud, legal, or executive cases, do not rely on a passive handoff. Escalate through a monitored partner path.

### Prevent circular referrals

If the partner sends the customer back, the original team needs a resolution path: escalation owner, policy review, manual exception, marketplace enforcement, or documented limitation. The customer should not bounce indefinitely between parties.

Track cases where partner routing repeatedly fails. That is an operational defect, not just an individual ticket issue.

### Close only after the next path is real

Closing is appropriate when the customer has a confirmed partner case, clear instructions, or a completed resolution. It is not appropriate when the agent merely tells the customer to try another party with no reference, timeline, or tracking.

Where the platform remains accountable for marketplace trust, keep visibility until the partner outcome is known.

### Monitor partner quality and enforcement signals

Individual handoffs can reveal broader partner quality problems: repeated late shipments, refund avoidance, unsafe products, misleading listings, poor installation work, abusive seller messages, warranty evasion, counterfeit concerns, or systematic refusal to cooperate. Capture these patterns and route them to marketplace operations, partner management, trust and safety, legal, or compliance as appropriate.

Support should not treat every partner failure as a one-off if customers are repeatedly harmed. The handoff record should preserve enough detail to support coaching, enforcement, delisting, contract review, or policy change.

### Clarify customer consent for partner contact

If the support team will ask a partner to contact the customer directly, confirm what contact information may be shared and what the customer should expect. Some customers do not want a seller, contractor, or local partner to receive additional personal details beyond what is necessary.

## Common Traps

- Redirecting the customer before confirming who owns the purchase, contract, or support obligation.
- Explaining partner boundaries in internal terms the customer cannot act on.
- Handing off without evidence, prior actions, commitments, or urgency.
- Sharing customer data with a seller or partner without consent or authority.
- Sending urgent safety, fraud, legal, or executive issues through ordinary partner channels; giving the customer no reference number, expected channel, or timing
- Closing as soon as another party is mentioned; letting the customer bounce between platform and partner with no escalation owner
- Ignoring seller or partner patterns that create repeated customer harm; failing to route repeated partner failures into marketplace enforcement, partner management, or policy review
- Sharing customer contact information for direct partner follow-up without checking consent and necessity; assuming marketplace policy always matches customer fairness

## Self-Check

- Is ownership of transaction, contract, warranty, refund, fulfillment, technical fix, data access, or service delivery identified?
- Has the customer's purchase path, account, order, seller, region, subscription source, and warranty status been verified?
- Does the handoff include issue summary, goal, evidence, IDs, prior actions, policy basis, urgency, commitments, and next decision?
- Is customer data shared only with appropriate consent, authority, and channel?
- Does the customer know who will respond, where, when, and what they may need to provide?
- Is there a monitored path for urgent, safety, fraud, legal, or executive cases?
- Is there a plan if the partner sends the customer back?
- Are repeated partner routing failures tracked as operational signals?
- Is the case closed only after the next path is real and usable?; does the handoff reduce customer effort rather than shifting work onto them?
- Are partner quality, enforcement, safety, counterfeit, abuse, or warranty-evasion signals captured?; is customer consent and data minimization handled before partner direct contact?
